# Transcrição: 17-Sleepwalking Thru Kaleigh s Surgery

- **Fonte:** 17-Sleepwalking Thru Kaleigh s Surgery
- **Duração:** 06:40
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

I'm standing in my classroom, it's about half an hour before the school day starts when my phone rings. I answer it and the woman on the other end of the phone tells me that she has good news. That my dog has survived the first surgery and she's getting ready to have the second surgery. And I tell the lady, no, you've called the wrong person. My dog is at the hospital but my dog is just constipated and being watched in case she explodes in some horrible way. And the woman says, oh my gosh, I'm so sorry. She says, could you hold for a moment? And she clicks off and when she does, I think about how someone in the world is now waiting to hear if their dog survived surgery and I just can't imagine it. My dog's name is Kaylee. She's a white lasso opso who I've had for three years and she's my best friend. I just, I can't imagine getting that phone call. So the woman clicks back and she says, is your name Matthew Dix? And I say, yeah. She says, is your dog Kaylee? And I say, yes. And she says, Mr. Dix, we just did spinal surgery on your dog. And I say, no you didn't because she's just constipated. Unless you did surgery on the wrong dog, there's a problem here. And she says, I'm so sorry. Can I just hold for one more second? And she clicks off and now I'm thinking like, who is taking care of my dog? And when they click back on, it's not the woman anymore. And I say, I'm a man. He says, my name is Dr. Lindgren. Are you Matthew Dix? Yes. Is your dog Kaylee? Yes. He says, Mr. Dix, I just did spinal surgery on your dog and I'm about to begin a second surgery. And I say, that's not possible. And he says, Mr. Dix, last night I called you at two o'clock in the morning and told you your dog wasn't constipated. She had a spinal rupture and you agreed to the surgery. And instantly I know exactly what has happened. And so I say, can I call you back in a minute? And he says, no, I need to do surgery on your dog right now or she will die. He gives me a number to call and I hang up and then I run. I run out of my classroom and up the hallway. I run to my wife's classroom. I didn't see my wife this morning because she sleeps in and I leave early. And so as I ran into her classroom, it's usually the time she's coming in. She's walking right in the door, her hands are full of bags and she sees me and she says, how's Kaylee? And I say, you know? And she said, yeah, how is she? Did she survive? And I said, she had surgery and she says, yes, you don't know. And now I know for sure. I have been sleepwalking for my entire life. And when people talk about sleepwalking, what they usually think is people wandering around a house, banging into walls, but that is not what it is like. I compare it as having two operating systems in my head. I've got an operating system that's running right now allowing me to tell this story, but there's a second one that comes online sometimes, about once a month. And it is capable of doing all the things that this one is capable of doing, except they don't talk to each other. And so I have no memory of the things that I do when I'm sleepwalking. When I was a kid, I would wander down a couple hours after going to bed,

## [03:03 - 06:05]

and I'd sit with my parents and watch the Celtics with them. And they would ask me questions like, so what girl do you like now, Matt? And I'd say, Melissa Zeritny. And then in the morning they'd say, so who's Melissa Zeritny? And I'd be like, fuck. And when I was in Boy Scouts, the Boy Scout, the Scoutmaster used to have to time my ankle to a tree, because if not, I would wander out of the tent and just walk into the woods until I tripped on a root, woke up, had no idea where I was, and would have to sit at the base of a tree until everyone woke up in the morning and I figured out where camp was. Nowadays when I sleepwalk, I mostly eat cereal at night. And I only know this because I come down in the morning and I find half-eaten bowls of Cheerios. And sometimes I find a book open next to the Cheerios as if I was reading, but I have no recollection of it. Last year I signed three book contracts, which means I had to write three books in a year, which was the worst decision I've ever made in my life, and there was an enormous amount of stress on me. So one morning I came down, I found my laptop open and 500 words written for the book I was working on. It wasn't the 500 words that matched up with what I was writing the night before, it was 500 words for the next chapter. But they're still the same 500 words that exist in the book today. The things I can do when I sleepwalk are sort of astounding, and my wife knows it. We have long conversations in the middle of the night that I have no recollection of and strike fear into my heart in a way you can't imagine. So she says, you were sleepwalking? And I said, I guess. And she said, we turned on the lights, we talked about it for an hour. And I said, well, what's the deal? And she said, the doctor told us that she needed surgery and there was a 50% chance of her surviving the first one. And then she needed a second surgery to stabilize the spine. That's the one happening right now, and there's a 50% chance of surviving that one. And even if she survives both of them, she's probably going to end up in a doggie wheelchair and we're going to have to catheter her and give her a colostomy bag for the rest of her life. And the surgery that's happening right now costs $8,000. And I say, we agreed to all of that? And she says, yeah. And suddenly I fall more in love with my wife than I ever have, because that dog is mine first and she is not easy. And the fact that she agreed to all of that, frankly, I cannot believe it. And so I get a call later on while I'm teaching my students, I pick up the phone and it's the doctor and he says she survived the surgery. And I say, is she going to walk? And he says, I don't think so. So three days later, I get to bring Kaylee home and she is a waste. She is shaven and falling apart. And the worst thing is she looks at me, she doesn't know why this has happened to her and I can't tell her. And it breaks my heart. And we're sitting there watching television that first night and she's on a towel and she's asleep. And I see her wake up and look at me and then she gets up on her feet and she walks towards me. I call the doctor and I say she's walking and he says that's impossible. She can't do that right now.

## [06:05 - 06:40]

And I say, I know, but she is. Kaylee died last summer and she lived for 18 years and they were the 18 best years of my life. She was my best friend. And when people ask me if I had been awake, would I have made that same decision? I understand why they ask because it's a legitimate question. That was a lot of money and very little chance of surviving. But I always say the same thing. I say yes, I would have made the same decision. I would have made it in my sleep. Thank you. Thank you.
